vitics_knowledge_base.project: Vitics — Nigeria AI Energy Intelligence Platform

document.title: National Renewable Energy Action Plans (NREAP) 2015–2030
document.adopted_by: Inter-Ministerial Committee on Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency (ICREEE)
document.ministry: Federal Ministry of Power, Works & Housing
document.framework: ECOWAS Renewable Energy Policy (EREP); SE4All Initiative

vision_statement.label: Electricity Vision 30:30:30
vision_statement.description: 30,000 MW of power by 2030, with at least 30% renewable energy in the electricity mix

solar_pv_trajectory.label: Solar PV Installed Capacity Targets
solar_pv_trajectory.note: Zero solar PV existed on the grid in 2010 and 2014

cooking_energy_targets.label: Domestic Cooking Energy Transformation
cooking_energy_targets.note: Modern fuel alternatives include LPG, biogas, solar cookers, kerosene

feed_in_tariff.label: Feed-in Tariff (FiT) Scheme
feed_in_tariff.eligible_size_range: 1 MW to 10 MW (hydro/wind); 1 MW to 5 MW (solar PV, biomass)
feed_in_tariff.review_cycle: Minor review annually; major review every 5 years

rural_electrification_fund.label: Rural Electrification Fund (REF)
rural_electrification_fund.grant_type: Capital grants for project start-up costs only (not operational/maintenance)
rural_electrification_fund.managing_body: REF Management Directorate within Rural Electrification Agency (REA)

renewable_energy_subsidy.label: Renewable Energy Subsidy and Grant
renewable_energy_subsidy.level: Up to 30% of initial costs of RE utilisation facilities
renewable_energy_subsidy.eligible: Communities, enterprises, and individuals embarking on RE generation projects

investment_tracking.finding: All investment data (Table 20) listed as NA across every technology and every year from 2010 to 2030
investment_tracking.consumer_implication: No financial accountability existed. Consumers could not verify if money was being deployed toward RE targets.

monitoring_system.finding: Explicitly stated: 'A monitoring system is not yet in place'
monitoring_system.consumer_implication: Targets had no enforcement. No independent body could verify progress or hold government accountable.

policies_not_active.finding: At time of publication, the following were still 'planned' with no active status: FiT regulation, Competitive Procurement Program, Rural Electrification Strategy (RESIP), Soft loans via Bank of Industry, 30% RE subsidy scheme, Building Code RE integration
policies_not_active.consumer_implication: The policy document was largely a plan to make plans. Investors and communities had no bankable instruments to act on.

zero_solar_baseline.finding: Zero MW of solar PV, wind, bioenergy, or geothermal existed on the national grid in 2010 or 2014
zero_solar_baseline.consumer_implication: The promised 2,000 MW solar PV by 2020 required building an entire industry from scratch in 6 years with no investment data and no launched FiT.

no_cookstove_standards.finding: 'No national standard for improved cookstoves has been developed so far. No public body has been assigned the responsibility.'
no_cookstove_standards.consumer_implication: The plan targeted 40% cookstove adoption by 2020 with no standards body, no certification, and no quality assurance for consumers buying cookstoves.

no_installer_certification.finding: RE installer certification schemes: 'No they are not in place yet'
no_installer_certification.consumer_implication: Consumers purchasing solar systems had no assurance that installers were qualified. Document itself notes stakeholders were 'disappointed by unsatisfactory performance and durability'.

voluntary_disco_obligations.finding: Distribution companies' (DisCos) renewable energy obligations were 'voluntary at the moment'
voluntary_disco_obligations.consumer_implication: The companies delivering electricity to consumers had no legal duty to source renewable energy. The 52% RE target by 2020 had no binding mechanism at the distribution level.

no_legal_consequence_for_noncompliance.finding: Rural electrification non-fulfilment: 'no legal and administrative consequences would ensue'
no_legal_consequence_for_noncompliance.consumer_implication: Rural Nigerians had no legal recourse if electrification promises were broken. Non-compliance was acknowledged as consequence-free.

renewable_baseline_at_publication.finding: Only 0.8% of electricity from renewables in 2012. Target was 16% by 2030 under NREEEP 2015.
renewable_baseline_at_publication.consumer_implication: Starting from near-zero with no investment tracking, no enforcement, and key instruments still 'under preparation' made the gap between promise and delivery structurally very wide.

soncap_quality_gap.finding: SONCAP (offshore certification before import) was the only quality safeguard for RE equipment
soncap_quality_gap.consumer_implication: Once equipment cleared import, there was no in-country inspection, no certified installer network, and no consumer complaints mechanism described.

consumer_plain_language_truths: You were promised 75% electricity access by 2020 — but the government's own investment tracking was completely blank when this plan was published.
The solar revolution was starting from zero. Not one megawatt of solar PV existed on the national grid two years before this document promised 100 MW.
The Feed-in Tariff meant to attract renewable energy investors hadn't launched yet — at the moment this document promised a 2016 launch.
Your dependence on generators and kerosene was officially acknowledged. Reducing it was a stated goal, but no monitoring system existed to track progress.
If the government failed to electrify rural areas, there were no legal consequences. Non-compliance was explicitly described as consequence-free.
If you bought solar equipment, there were no certified installers and no quality standards body. The document itself admitted consumers were disappointed by poor-quality RE products.
97.66% of Nigerians were still relying on kerosene, LPG, or wood for cooking in 2010. The plan promised to change this by 2030 — with no assigned standards body and no cookstove certification scheme.

key_actors.NERC: Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission — responsible for licensing, FiT regulation, tariffs
key_actors.REA: Rural Electrification Agency — responsible for rural electrification delivery
key_actors.NBET: Nigerian Bulk Electricity Trader — manages competitive procurement, buys from IPPs
key_actors.TCN: Transmission Company of Nigeria — grid operation and transmission
key_actors.NAPTIN: National Power Training Institute of Nigeria — RE capacity building
key_actors.SON: Standard Organisation of Nigeria — equipment standards (SONCAP)
key_actors.ICREEE: Inter-Ministerial Committee on Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency — policy coordination
key_actors.ECN: Energy Commission of Nigeria — biomass and biofuel research/methodology
key_actors.GIZ_NESP: GIZ Nigerian Energy Support Programme — key development partner, FiT and grid code support

regional_context.nigeria_contribution: 8% of energy from renewables by 2020; 16% by 2030
